title: Insertion Sort
whenToUse: Reference iterative sort. First example in class. Model for loop-invariant proof structure.
keyFormulas: Loop invariant: after iteration $j$ of the outer loop, $A[1..j]$ is sorted
professor:
**Algorithm.**

$$ \texttt{InsertionSort}(A, n): \text{ for } j \text{ from } 1 \text{ to } n: \text{ for } i \text{ from } j \text{ down to } 2: \text{ if } A[i] < A[i-1] \text{ swap}.\ \text{Return } A. $$

**Loop invariant.** After the $j$-th iteration of the outer loop, $A[1..j]$ is sorted in increasing order.

**Correctness.**

- Initialization ($j = 1$): $A[1..1]$ is trivially sorted.
- Maintenance: assume $A[1..j]$ is sorted. In iteration $j+1$, the inner loop takes $A[j+1]$ and swaps it leftward until it is in the correct position relative to $A[1..j]$ — this is exactly 'insert $A[j+1]$ into its sorted place'. So after iteration $j+1$, $A[1..j+1]$ is sorted.
- Termination: at $j = n$, $A[1..n]$ is sorted.

**Worst-case runtime.** Outer loop runs $n$ times; inner loop runs at most $j - 1$ times per outer iteration; constant work per inner iteration. Total $\sum_{j=1}^{n} (j-1) \cdot c = c \cdot n(n-1)/2 = \Theta(n^2)$.

**Best case.** If input is already sorted, inner loop does zero comparisons (no swap triggered). $\Theta(n)$.

**Why it's worth knowing.** The loop-invariant structure (Initialization / Maintenance / Termination) is the canonical template for proving correctness of iterative algorithms.
simplified:
**Algorithm:** walk left-to-right. At step $j$, slide $A[j]$ leftward via swaps until it reaches its correct place in $A[1..j]$.

**Runtime:**

- Worst case: $\Theta(n^2)$ — array sorted in reverse.
- Best case: $\Theta(n)$ — already sorted.

**Loop invariant:** 'After iteration $j$, $A[1..j]$ is sorted.' This is the first example of the loop-invariant proof pattern the professor uses throughout.
